Q1: My study routine was honestly pretty unconventional. I couldn't attend classes regularly due to work and other commitments, so I crammed my studying into intense bursts, usually late at night or early mornings. I'm a visual learner, so I'd spend hours watching the lecture videos and creating detailed mind maps and color-coded notes. It wasn't consistent week-to-week, but when I did study, I made every minute count.
Q2: My pattern varied wildly depending on what was happening in my life. Some weeks I barely touched anything, and then other weeks I'd put in marathon sessions. Deadlines definitely drove a lot of my activity—I'd calculate exactly how much time I needed and work backward. The anxiety of falling behind actually pushed me to be more strategic about when and how I studied.
Q3: Usually it was either to watch lecture videos I'd missed or to check if there were any announcements about assignments or exams. I'd also log in when I felt that familiar anxiety creeping up, like I needed to reassure myself I wasn't completely lost. Sometimes my peers would mention something in our group chat, and I'd hop on to see what they were talking about.
Q4: Not really a daily routine, but checking in became almost a stress response for me. When my anxiety spiked about the course, I'd log in just to see where I stood. Around mid-semester, I started making it a Sunday evening ritual to watch all the videos I'd missed and plan my week. It helped me feel more in control even when attendance wasn't possible.
Q5: The lecture videos were absolutely essential for me—I watched nearly 158 minutes worth and often rewatched sections multiple times. As a visual learner, seeing concepts explained with diagrams and slides made everything click. I also found the peer interactions valuable; connecting with 30 different classmates helped me fill in gaps and stay motivated when I couldn't be there in person.
Q6: I completely ignored the discussion forums—never posted once. They felt time-consuming and I was already stretched thin, so I prioritized what directly impacted my grades. Some of the supplementary readings I skipped too, not because they weren't valuable, but because I had to make hard choices about where to invest my limited time.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to assessment criteria or helped me understand core concepts visually. I'd ask myself: will this help me on the exam? Will this make the assignment better? If the answer was yes, I engaged deeply. If it felt like busywork or something that wouldn't move the needle on my grades, I had to let it go.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford. With my anxiety levels and limited time, I had to be ruthless about prioritization. Required activities got my full attention and effort—that's why my assignment scores were so high. Optional stuff always got pushed to 'maybe later,' which usually meant never.
Q9: Absolutely, most of my studying happened offline. I'd download the lecture slides and videos, then study them on my tablet during commutes or work breaks. I created extensive handwritten notes with diagrams and flowcharts—very visual stuff that helped me process information. I also used YouTube videos and external resources to supplement concepts I found confusing.
Q10: There were entire weeks where I was deeply engaged with the material but barely touched the LMS. I'd be reviewing my printed notes, redrawing concept maps, or working through practice problems from a textbook. My study group met in person sometimes, and those sessions were incredibly productive but left no digital trace. The LMS only captured a fraction of my actual learning.
Q11: Yes, this was basically my standard approach. I'd download everything in one session—videos, slides, readings—and then work through them over the following days without logging back in. It was more efficient for me to have everything accessible offline, especially since my internet access wasn't always reliable when I was studying at odd hours or locations.
Q12: There were definitely dark periods, usually when my anxiety peaked and everything felt overwhelming. Around midterms, I had some personal stuff going on, and my attendance dropped even lower than usual. The CBT sessions I was doing helped me develop coping strategies, but there were weeks where just opening the LMS felt like too much. My resilience eventually kicked in, but those dips were real.
Q13: Competing responsibilities were my biggest challenge—that's why my attendance was under 20%. Fatigue hit hard during intense study sessions, and sometimes I'd have the LMS open but my brain was just not absorbing anything. Confusion about certain topics made me anxious, which paradoxically made me avoid those sections temporarily. I had to actively fight against that avoidance pattern.
Q14: Definitely. Sometimes I'd watch a video while exhausted and realize at the end that I'd retained nothing. I'd have to rewatch it later when I was more alert. There were also times I scrolled through materials just to feel like I was doing something, but I wasn't really processing the information. My high anxiety sometimes made it hard to focus even when I was technically 'engaged.'
Q15: I postponed discussion posts indefinitely because they triggered my anxiety about being judged by peers. Some complex topics I avoided until absolutely necessary because I feared not understanding them. Even knowing these were important, my brain would find reasons to tackle 'easier' tasks first. The CBT techniques I learned helped me recognize this pattern, but breaking it was a constant struggle.
Q16: Deadlines were my primary engine. I'd calculate exactly when I needed to start something to finish it well, then execute intensely. My assignment completion rate was low overall, but the ones I did submit scored really high because I focused all my energy on them. Without deadlines, I probably would have drifted—they gave structure to my chaotic schedule.
Q17: Feedback was incredibly motivating for me. When I saw high scores on my assignments, it validated that my intense but irregular approach was working. It reduced my anxiety about my unconventional study habits. Positive feedback made me more confident in prioritizing depth over breadth, and it reinforced that I could succeed despite low attendance.
Q18: The course structure was both helpful and challenging for me. Having recorded lectures available was essential since I couldn't attend in person. However, the expectation of regular participation in discussions didn't fit my situation at all. I appreciated that the assessments weighted heavily toward exams and assignments rather than attendance or participation.
Q19: The availability of video content made engagement possible for someone like me with attendance challenges. However, the lack of flexibility in some deadlines and the emphasis on synchronous participation made things harder. If more activities had been designed for asynchronous completion, I could have engaged more fully. The visual materials were excellent though—they really suited my learning style.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is anxious or uncertain about what they're supposed to do. They might keep checking materials, rewatching videos, searching for clarity, but never feel confident enough to actually submit work. It could also be perfectionism—wanting everything to be perfect before submitting. I experienced some of this myself, though I eventually pushed through.
Q21: This absolutely reflects my experience. My LMS activity was moderate, but my exam score was nearly 90 and I got a Distinction. I did most of my deep learning offline with downloaded materials and personal notes. The LMS captured my video watching and peer interactions, but missed all the hours I spent with physical notes and external resources. Low activity doesn't mean low engagement.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show login patterns, time spent on videos, and submission timestamps. But they completely miss offline study, the quality of engagement during online sessions, emotional state while learning, and peer interactions that happen outside the platform. They can't capture whether someone understood something or just clicked through it. They're a partial picture at best.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely wrestling with the material until it makes sense, not just going through the motions. For me, it's about creating those visual connections, asking questions even if just to myself, and caring about truly understanding rather than just passing. It's mental investment, not just time investment. Some of my most engaged moments happened completely offline.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show my anxiety levels and how much emotional energy it took to stay on track. It wouldn't capture the CBT work I did to manage my stress around the course. It wouldn't show the elaborate visual study systems I created offline, or the genuine intellectual excitement I felt when concepts clicked. Most importantly, it wouldn't show that despite appearing minimally involved, I was deeply committed to succeeding.
